sango.title: Sango, The Thunder King
sango.summary: Sango was a harsh and violent Alaafin of the Oyo Empire, a warrior king so powerful that after his tragic death, he was deified as the Orisha of thunder and lightning.
key_facts: Sango was a real historical king of Oyo before he became a deity.
He sprang from the body of the Orisha Yemoja.
He relentlessly searched for power through magic, eventually obtaining dominion over thunder.
His power destroyed his own palace, killing his wife and children inside.
Dismayed by his catastrophic mistake, he fled his kingdom and hanged himself.
He was the husband of Oshun, and together they produced the sacred Ibeji (twins).
tensions: A king so desperate for power that his magic destroyed the family he loved.
The tragedy of a mortal ruler who could not control the divine forces he summoned.
He chose death by his own hand over living with the dishonor of his actions.

proverbs.english: When fire burns, Sango begs forgiveness
proverbs.meaning: Even the most powerful forces must reckon with the consequences of their strength.

sango.story_hook: A king who could call lightning from the sky lost everything to a single flame he could not control. How does a god fall?

olodumare.title: Olofin, The Supreme Creator
olodumare.summary: The genderless, all-knowing, and eternal Supreme Being who crafted the universe, invented day and night, and breathed life into every living thing.
key_facts: Olofin crafted the universe and lives in the distant heavenly realm (Orun).
They gave Ase (divine energy) to all objects and Emi (the breath of life) to all living things.
Olofin created the Orisha to act as intermediaries because they did not want to manage every living thing directly.
When the Orisha rebelled, Olofin simply held back the rain, proving their supreme power through silence rather than violence.
tensions: A creator so vast and powerful that they are too distant to hear the direct cries of humanity.
The rebellion of the lesser gods who thought they could rule the earth without the Supreme Being's influence.

proverbs.yoruba: Àìgbagbọ́ nínú Ọlọ́run ni ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ wèrè
proverbs.english: Disbelief in God is the beginning of madness
proverbs.meaning: True wisdom begins with acknowledging the supreme force that holds the universe together.

olodumare.story_hook: Before there was land, before there were gods, there was a single breath that gave energy to the darkness.

obatala.title: Obatala, The Creator of Forms
obatala.summary: The primordial Orisha who requested permission to create land on Earth and was tasked with molding the first human beings out of clay.
key_facts: Obatala used a long gold chain forged from the spare gold of other gods to climb down from the sky.
He poured sand from a snail shell and dropped a chicken, which scattered the sand to create landmasses.
He planted a date palm nut that sprouted instantly, marking the spot of Ile-Ife.
He got drunk on palm wine and molded imperfect humans with missing limbs or bends.
Upon seeing his mistake, he vowed never to drink again and became the protector of humans with physical differences.
tensions: A god's drunken mistake forever altered the physical form of humanity.
The deep remorse of a creator who must spend eternity protecting the broken creations he made.

proverbs.meaning: Every person, regardless of their physical form, carries the divine breath.

obatala.story_hook: We are all molded from the same earth, but it was a god's drunken mistake that taught the heavens compassion.

oshun.title: Oshun, The River of Sacrifice
oshun.summary: The youngest of the Orisha, who rules the sweet waters. She saved the world from a devastating drought by sacrificing her unmatched beauty to reach the Supreme Creator.
key_facts: When Olofin withheld the rain, the other gods were too weak or tired to reach the distant heavens.
Oshun turned into a beautiful peacock and flew toward the sun to plead for humanity.
The journey burned away all her magnificent feathers, turning her into an ugly vulture.
Olofin was so touched by her courage that he brought the rain back and decreed that only the vulture could communicate directly with him.
tensions: The youngest and most doubted deity succeeding where the ancient, powerful gods failed.
Sacrificing the ultimate symbol of beauty and vanity for the survival of the earth.

proverbs.yoruba: Omi òtá inú, omi òtá ìta
proverbs.meaning: Purity, necessity, and grace ultimately overcome all resistance.

oshun.story_hook: What is the price of rain? Once, the most beautiful of the gods had to burn away her beauty so the world could drink.

eshu.title: Eshu, The Divine Trickster
eshu.summary: The messenger Orisha who stands watch at the boundary between the realms of Orun (heaven) and Aye (earth), acting as the ultimate gatekeeper of prayers and sacrifices.
key_facts: During the great flood, the humans begged Eshu, not the Supreme Creator, to intervene and save them.
He ferries notes and sacrifices across the threshold between the human and divine worlds.
If Eshu is not acknowledged first during a ritual, the ritual is useless.
tensions: He holds the keys to heaven, yet he thrives on chaos and trickery.
Without his approval, the prayers of the most righteous humans will fall on deaf ears.

proverbs.yoruba: Bí a bá rúbọ, kí a fún Èṣù ní tirè
proverbs.english: If we sacrifice, let us give Eshu his portion
proverbs.meaning: Always acknowledge the gatekeeper, or your efforts will be scattered to the wind.

eshu.story_hook: Before you speak to the heavens, you must pay the keeper of the gate. For if you ignore the trickster, your words are just wind.

olokun.title: Olokun, The Wrath of the Deep
olokun.summary: The formidable Orisha who ruled over the vast, primordial ocean before the earth was formed, known for unleashing a catastrophic flood when disrespected.
key_facts: Olokun was furious that Obatala encroached upon her ocean to build land without asking for her permission.
She sent great, devastating waves to flood the new land and drown its inhabitants.
Her flood could only be stopped by Orunmila casting spells from Obatala's golden chain.
tensions: A primordial force striking back violently when her domain is stolen.
The deep sea refusing to yield to the land without a war.

proverbs.meaning: Never forget who holds true dominion over the depths.

olokun.story_hook: Before the earth, there was only the endless dark water. And the ocean does not forgive those who steal from it.

orunmila.title: Orunmila, The Witness of Destiny
orunmila.summary: The Orisha of prophecy and divination. Because of his immense wisdom, he was the sole witness when Olofin created the universe.
key_facts: He sits on the boundary of heaven and earth, allowing mortals to connect to universal forces through divination.
He guided Obatala on exactly what items to gather to create the earth.
He is the one who climbed down the chain and cast spells to force Olokun's great flood to retreat.
tensions: He knows the destiny of every living thing, but must watch mortals make their own mistakes.
The burden of holding the secrets of the universe's creation.

proverbs.meaning: Foresight and knowledge can calm the greatest storms and defeat the strongest enemies.

orunmila.story_hook: There is only one pair of eyes that watched the universe being born. And those eyes can see the end of your story, too.

atunwa.title: Atunwa, The Cycle of Rebirth
atunwa.summary: The deep Yoruba philosophy and belief system surrounding death and rebirth, ensuring that souls remain intricately tied to their bloodlines.
key_facts: Unlike karmic reincarnation where a soul can return in any random form, Atunwa dictates you will be reborn strictly into your own family.
Someone who lived a long, good life joins the ancestors in Orun (heaven) and may be reincarnated by Olofin's plan.
Your actions in your current life directly determine your family's future fortune.
If you misbehave, you will not come back as an insect; you will be born into poverty as your own great-grandson.
tensions: You are the architect of your own future suffering or prosperity.
The inescapable tie of blood; you can never outrun the legacy you leave behind.

proverbs.yoruba: Bí abá ti ṣe ni a ó rí, bí a bá gbin èbù, a ó kórè èbù
proverbs.english: As we do, so we shall see; if we plant yams, we shall harvest yams
proverbs.meaning: Your current actions are the seeds of your own future rebirth.

atunwa.story_hook: Do not weep too hard for the dead. If they lived well, they will simply return to us in the eyes of the next child.

general.title: The Yoruba Cosmos
general.summary: The Yoruba people possess a vibrant, dynamic culture that stretches back almost 2,000 years, bound by Isese, the traditional philosophy and way of life.
key_facts: The Yoruba cosmos is divided into two halves: Orun (the spiritual heavenly realm) and Aye (the tangible, visible world).
The culture is preserved through Isese, a philosophy and religion still practiced today.
There are roughly 50 million people speaking the language across Nigeria, Benin, Ghana, and Togo.
The mythology strongly influences Caribbean spiritual practices like Santeria and Candomble.

proverbs.meaning: A person's true worth is measured by their character, not their appearance.

general.story_hook: Before there were borders drawn on maps, there was a people whose stories shaped the heavens and the earth.
